title: Infimum and supremum
content:
In mathematics, the infimum (abbreviated inf; plural infima) of a subset formula_1 of a partially ordered set formula_2 is the greatest element in formula_2 that is less than or equal to all elements of formula_4 if such an element exists. Consequently, the term "greatest lower bound" (abbreviated as) is also commonly used. The supremum (abbreviated sup; plural suprema) of a subset formula_1 of a partially ordered set formula_2 is the least element in formula_2 that is greater than or equal to all elements of formula_4 if such an element exists.

Consequently, the supremum is also referred to as the "least upper bound" (or). The infimum is in a precise sense dual to the concept of a supremum. Infima and suprema of real number s are common special cases that are important in analysis, and especially in Lebesgue integration. However, the general definitions remain valid in the more abstract setting of order theory where arbitrary partially ordered sets are considered. The concepts of infimum and supremum are similar to minimum and maximum, but are more useful in analysis because they better characterize special sets which may have.

For instance, the set of positive real numbers formula_9 (not including formula_10) does not have a minimum, because any given element of formula_9 could simply be divided in half resulting in a smaller number that is still in formula_12 There is, however, exactly one infimum of the positive real numbers: formula_13 which is smaller than all the positive real numbers and greater than any other real number which could be used as a lower bound. Formal definition.

A of a subset formula_1 of a partially ordered set formula_15 is an element formula_16 of formula_17 such that A lower bound formula_16 of formula_1 is called an (or, or) of formula_1 if Similarly, an of a subset formula_1 of a partially ordered set formula_15 is an element formula_30 of formula_17 such that An upper bound formula_30 of formula_1 is called a (or, or) of formula_1 if Existence and uniqueness. Infima and suprema do not necessarily exist.

Existence of an infimum of a subset formula_1 of formula_17 can fail if formula_1 has no lower bound at all, or if the set of lower bounds does not contain a greatest element. However, if an infimum or supremum does exist, it is unique. Consequently, partially ordered sets for which certain infima are known to exist become especially interesting. For instance, a lattice is a partially ordered set in which all subsets have both a supremum and an infimum, and a complete lattice is a partially ordered set in which subsets have both a supremum and an infimum.

More information on the various classes of partially ordered sets that arise from such considerations are found in the article on completeness properties. If the supremum of a subset formula_1 exists, it is unique. If formula_1 contains a greatest element, then that element is the supremum; otherwise, the supremum does not belong to formula_1 (or does not exist). Likewise, if the infimum exists, it is unique. If formula_1 contains a least element, then that element is the infimum; otherwise, the infimum does not belong to formula_1 (or does not exist). Relation to maximum and minimum elements.

The infimum of a subset formula_1 of a partially ordered set formula_25 assuming it exists, does not necessarily belong to formula_52 If it does, it is a minimum or least element of formula_52 Similarly, if the supremum of formula_1 belongs to formula_4 it is a maximum or greatest element of formula_52 For example, consider the set of negative real numbers (excluding zero). This set has no greatest element, since for every element of the set, there is another, larger, element. For instance, for any negative real number formula_57 there is another negative real number formula_58 which is greater.

On the other hand, every real number greater than or equal to zero is certainly an upper bound on this set. Hence, formula_10 is the least upper bound of the negative reals, so the supremum is 0. This set has a supremum but no greatest element. However, the definition of maximal and minimal elements is more general. In particular, a set can have many maximal and minimal elements, whereas infima and suprema are unique.

Whereas maxima and minima must be members of the subset that is under consideration, the infimum and supremum of a subset need not be members of that subset themselves. Minimal upper bounds. Finally, a partially ordered set may have many minimal upper bounds without having a least upper bound. Minimal upper bounds are those upper bounds for which there is no strictly smaller element that also is an upper bound. This does not say that each minimal upper bound is smaller than all other upper bounds, it merely is not greater.

The distinction between "minimal" and "least" is only possible when the given order is not a total one. In a totally ordered set, like the real numbers, the concepts are the same. As an example, let formula_1 be the set of all finite subsets of natural numbers and consider the partially ordered set obtained by taking all sets from formula_1 together with the set of integer s formula_62 and the set of positive real numbers formula_63 ordered by subset inclusion as above. Then clearly both formula_62 and formula_9 are greater than all finite sets of natural numbers.

Yet, neither is formula_9 smaller than formula_62 nor is the converse true: both sets are minimal upper bounds but none is a supremum. Least-upper-bound property. The is an example of the aforementioned completeness properties which is typical for the set of real numbers. This property is sometimes called. If an ordered set formula_1 has the property that every nonempty subset of formula_1 having an upper bound also has a least upper bound, then formula_1 is said to have the least-upper-bound property. As noted above, the set formula_71 of all real numbers has the least-upper-bound property.

Similarly, the set formula_62 of integers has the least-upper-bound property; if formula_1 is a nonempty subset of formula_62 and there is some number formula_75 such that every element formula_76 of formula_1 is less than or equal to formula_78 then there is a least upper bound formula_79 for formula_4 an integer that is an upper bound for formula_1 and is less than or equal to every other upper bound for formula_52 A well-order ed set also has the least-upper-bound property, and the empty subset has also a least upper bound: the minimum of the whole set.

An example of a set that the least-upper-bound property is formula_83 the set of rational numbers. Let formula_1 be the set of all rational numbers formula_85 such that formula_86 Then formula_1 has an upper bound (formula_88 for example, or formula_89) but no least upper bound in formula_90: If we suppose formula_91 is the least upper bound, a contradiction is immediately deduced because between any two reals formula_92 and formula_23 (including formula_94 and formula_95) there exists some rational formula_96 which itself would have to be the least upper bound (if formula_97) or a member of formula_1 greater than formula_95 (if formula_100).

Another example is the hyperreals; there is no least upper bound of the set of positive infinitesimals. There is a corresponding; an ordered set possesses the greatest-lower-bound property if and only if it also possesses the least-upper-bound property; the least-upper-bound of the set of lower bounds of a set is the greatest-lower-bound, and the greatest-lower-bound of the set of upper bounds of a set is the least-upper-bound of the set.

If in a partially ordered set formula_17 every bounded subset has a supremum, this applies also, for any set formula_102 in the function space containing all functions from formula_103 to formula_25 where formula_105 if and only if formula_106 for all formula_107 For example, it applies for real functions, and, since these can be considered special cases of functions, for real formula_75-tuples and sequences of real numbers. The least-upper-bound property is an indicator of the suprema. Infima and suprema of real numbers. In analysis, infima and suprema of subsets formula_1 of the real numbers are particularly important.

For instance, the negative real number s do not have a greatest element, and their supremum is formula_10 (which is not a negative real number). The completeness of the real numbers implies (and is equivalent to) that any bounded nonempty subset formula_1 of the real numbers has an infimum and a supremum. If formula_1 is not bounded below, one often formally writes formula_113 If formula_1 is empty, one writes formula_115 Properties.

In the last example, the supremum of a set of rationals is irrational, which means that the rationals are incomplete.
